children.name: Vombatus
children.description: Common wombats — barrel-shaped, powerfully built marsupials that are the world's largest burrowing herbivores. A wombat can dig a burrow tunnel over 30 m long and 2 m deep. Their backward-facing pouch (unique among marsupials) prevents soil from entering while digging. Wombats are the only animals known to produce cubic faeces — a result of non-uniform elasticity in the last section of the intestine, which shapes the droppings as they dry over a 2-3 week digestion time. The cubic shape prevents the droppings rolling away from marking posts, believed to be important in territorial communication. During the 2020 Australian bushfires, wombats reportedly allowed other animals to shelter in their burrows, leading to the romantic but unverified 'wombat hero' story.

children.name: Vombatus ursinus
children.description: The most widespread wombat, found in southeastern Australia including Tasmania. Can weigh up to 35 kg. Produces approximately 100 cubic faecal pellets per night. Despite its bulk, can run at 40 km/h for short distances.

children.name: Lasiorhinus
children.description: Hairy-nosed wombats — two species with soft, silky fur over their muzzle (distinguishing them from the bare-nosed common wombat). The southern hairy-nosed wombat is the most abundant wombat species overall. The northern hairy-nosed wombat is one of Australia's most critically endangered mammals: it survives in a single location, Epping Forest National Park in Queensland, with fewer than 250 individuals — the population was down to just 35 animals in 1981. Conservation efforts have allowed gradual recovery, and a second population has been established at Richard Underwood Nature Refuge.

children.name: Lasiorhinus latifrons
children.description: The most numerous wombat. Found in semi-arid southern Australia. Can survive without drinking water for months, deriving all moisture from vegetation. Warren systems may be occupied by generations for over 100 years.

children.name: Lasiorhinus krefftii
children.description: Critically endangered — one of Australia's rarest mammals. Down to just 35 individuals in 1981; recovered to approximately 250 after intensive conservation. A second population of around 15 was established in 2009. Larger than the southern hairy-nosed wombat.
